summary: Tiny bounded smoke test of the Why Tree engine (no web, ~4 agents)

result.apex: Why do most New Year's resolutions fail by February?

stages.summary: Resolutions are framed as outcome goals with no concrete cue-action-reward loop or implementation intention, so there is nothing for the brain to trigger.
Goals are set at a peak-motivation calendar spike (fresh-start effect) and over-commit; once motivation regresses to baseline by February the behavior is too costly to sustain.
Environment still cues old habits and the person still self-identifies as their old self; without restructuring either, defaults reassert.
Cost is front-loaded (Jan effort/deprivation) while reward is back-loaded (months out); the daily incentive gradient favors defection.
No external accountability/stake (internal willpower only, easily renegotiated) plus the social-signaling reward of declaring a 'new me' is collected up front at declaration.

Outcome-framing leaves no daily metric, so adherence silently drifts; the calendar-boundary motivation burst is unreinforced by feedback and fades in 4-6 weeks; too many/too ambitious goals overload willpower budget.

roots.root: Hyperbolic discounting / front-loaded cost vs back-loaded reward
roots.note: At every daily decision point the immediate payoff of skipping beats the discounted distant payoff of complying. This is the load-bearing mechanism the other roots feed into.

roots.root: No behavior-level loop or measurement (outcome-framing, no implementation intention, no tracking)
roots.note: Vague outcome goals provide no cue-action-reward to execute and no metric to notice drift.

roots.root: Motivation spike at calendar boundary regresses to baseline, unreinforced
roots.note: Fresh-start high + over-commitment, no feedback signal to sustain it past ~4-6 weeks.

roots.root: No external accountability / stake; symbolic reward collected at declaration
roots.note: Only depletable internal willpower enforces; declaring 'new me' prematurely satisfies the identity reward.
Old cues persist, old self-concept persists, too many goals exceed the budget. Weakest/most contested evidence.

constraint.the_constraint: Hyperbolic discounting — front-loaded cost vs back-loaded reward
constraint.why: It is the only A-grade root and the convergence point: every other root (no loop, decaying motivation, no stake, unchanged environment) is a way the daily defection-favoring incentive gradient goes unopposed. Fix the gradient (shrink/immediacy of cost, bring reward forward, add immediate stake) and the others become survivable. Leave it unfixed and willpower alone loses by February.
constraint.leverage: Add an immediate reward/stake at the daily decision point and shrink the per-day cost so compliance wins the present-biased comparison.

census.bottom_line: Honest read: this is a reasoning-only tree — zero OBSERVATIONs and zero FACTs, so nothing here is grounded in case-specific evidence; it is entirely priors from behavioral-science general knowledge. 6 of 9 branches are HYPOTHESIS-grade and only one branch (hyperbolic discounting) earns an A, and it earns it on logical force, not on data. The constraint call is theory-driven and would need real adherence/dropout data to confirm. Plumbing works; diagnosis is unverified by design.

negatives: Not primarily a knowledge/information gap — people know how to exercise or save; failure is at execution, not understanding.
Not mainly goal difficulty per se — even modest resolutions fail, so 'too hard' is contributing, not root.
Not a one-off motivation failure of weak-willed individuals — failure rate is near-universal, pointing to structural incentive design, not personal defect.
No evidence here that external shocks (illness, life events) drive the February cliff; the cliff tracks the motivation/incentive curve, not random events.

tests.test: Implementation-intention + immediacy A/B: have one cohort attach the behavior to a fixed daily cue with a same-day micro-reward, compare 6-week adherence vs plain resolution cohort.
tests.cost: cheap — self-report adherence diary, no instrumentation

tests.test: Stake test: small refundable financial deposit forfeited on a missed day (commitment device); compare February survival vs no-stake control.
tests.targets: no-accountability root + confirms front-loaded-cost mechanism by adding immediate cost to defection
